concepts.title: Chain Rule
concepts.conceptBody:
Differential coefficient of function of function

Meaning of ‘function of function’ 

Let $y={{\left( {{x}^{3}}+1 \right)}^{5}}$

Here ${{x}^{3}}+1$ is a function of $x$ and if $u={{x}^{3}}+1$ then $y={{u}^{5}}$.

Hence $\text{y}$ is a function of $\text{u}$.

Thus, we see that $\text{y}$ is a function of $\text{u}$ and $\text{u}$ is a function of $\text{x}$. In this, we say that $\text{y}$ is a function of function.

d.c. of function of function. 

Let $\text{y}$ be a differentiable function of $\text{u}$ and $\text{u}$ be a differentiable function of $\text{x}$,

then $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{du}\times \frac{du}{dx}$

Hence $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{du}\cdot \frac{du}{dx}$

Corollary. If $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{u} \right),\text{u}=\phi \left( \text{v} \right)$ and $\text{v}=\psi \left( \text{x} \right)$.

Then $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{du}\cdot \frac{du}{dv}\cdot \frac{dv}{dx}$

Chain Rule 

If $y$ and $x$ both are expressed in terms of $t$, then

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$

Take $y$ in place of $t$ in (b), we get: 

$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{dy} / \mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx} / \mathrm{dy}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{dx} / \mathrm{dy}}$

Hence derivative of $\text{y}$ w.r.t. $\text{x}$ and derivative of $\text{x}$ w.r.t. $\text{y}$ are reciprocal of each other.

Choosing which Rule to use 

The choice of which rules to use in solving a differentiation problem can make a difference in how much work you have to do. Here is an example.

Illustration: 

Rather than using the Quotient Rule to find the derivative of

$y=\frac{\left( x-1 \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}-2x \right)}{{{x}^{4}}}$

expand the numerator and divide by ${{x}^{4}}$.

$y=\frac{\left( x-1 \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}-2x \right)}{{{x}^{4}}}=\frac{{{x}^{3}}-3{{x}^{2}}+2x}{{{x}^{4}}}={{x}^{-1}}-3{{x}^{-2}}+2{{x}^{-3}}$

Then use the Sum and Power Rules:

Second and Higher Order Derivatives 

The derivative ${y}'=\frac{dy}{dx}$ is the first (first order) derivative of $y$ with respect to $x$. This derivative may itself be a differentiable function of $\text{x}$; if so, its derivative

${y}''=\frac{d{y}'}{dx}=\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)=\frac{{{d}^{2}}y}{d{{x}^{2}}}$

is called the second (second order) derivative of $\text{y}$ with respect to $\text{x}$.

If ${y}''$ is differentiable, its derivative, ${y}'''=\frac{d{y}''}{dx}=\frac{{{d}^{3}}y}{d{{x}^{3}}}$ is the third (third order) derivative of y with respect to $\text{x}$. The names continue as you imagine, with

${{y}^{\left( n \right)}}=\frac{d}{dx}{{y}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}$

denoting the $n$th (nth order) derivative of $\text{y}$ with respect to $\text{x}$, for any positive integer $\text{n}$.
